title: Eliminate load-use stall via MEM-to-EX forwarding
motivation:
The baseline 5-stage pipeline stalls for one cycle on every load-use
dependency because the load result is only available at the end of MEM,
while the dependent instruction is already in EX. CoreMark contains
frequent pointer-chasing and data-dependent loads, so these stalls are
a significant IPC bottleneck. Adding a combinational bypass from the
MEM-stage load data to the EX-stage operand mux removes the stall
entirely for the common single-cycle load case.

hypothesis.motivation:
The baseline 5-stage pipeline stalls for one cycle on every load-use
dependency because the load result is only available at the end of MEM,
while the dependent instruction is already in EX. CoreMark contains
frequent pointer-chasing and data-dependent loads, so these stalls are
a significant IPC bottleneck. Adding a combinational bypass from the
MEM-stage load data to the EX-stage operand mux removes the stall
entirely for the common single-cycle load case.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
1. Export the combinational load_data signal from mem_stage so it can
   be forwarded to EX.
2. Extend forward_unit with a third source (2'b11) selected when the
   instruction in EX/MEM is a load and its rd matches the EX-stage rs1
   or rs2.
3. Extend ex_stage with a new fwd_mem_load_data input and mux it in
   when the select is 2'b11.
4. Remove load_use_hazard from hazard_unit's stall/flush outputs so
   the dependent instruction proceeds into EX on the cycle immediately
   following the load.
5. Update test_pipeline.py to assert that the dependent instruction
   retires one cycle earlier (no load-use bubble).

changes.description: Add output port load_data_out driven from internal combinational load_data.
Add ex_mem_mem_read input; emit fwd select 2'd3 when forwarding from a load in MEM.
Add fwd_mem_load_data input and mux it in for select value 2'd3.
Remove load_use_hazard from stall_if, stall_id, and flush_id logic.
Wire mem_stage.load_data_out to ex_stage.fwd_mem_load_data and ex_mem_w.ctrl.mem_read to forward_unit.ex_mem_mem_read.
Update load_use_stalls test to assert the ADD retires on cycle 8 (no stall) instead of implicitly accepting a delayed retirement.
Add output port load_data_out driven from internal combinational load_data.
Add ex_mem_mem_read input; emit fwd select 2'd3 when forwarding from a load in MEM.
Add fwd_mem_load_data input and mux it in for select value 2'd3.
Remove load_use_hazard from stall_if, stall_id, and flush_id logic.
Wire mem_stage.load_data_out to ex_stage.fwd_mem_load_data and ex_mem_w.ctrl.mem_read to forward_unit.ex_mem_mem_read.
Update load_use_stalls test to assert the ADD retires on cycle 8 (no stall) instead of implicitly accepting a delayed retirement.

title: Replace combinational divider with iterative 32-cycle unit
motivation:
The baseline ALU implements DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU using combinational Verilog
`/` and `%` operators.  On the Tang Nano 20K this infers a massive
combinational subtract-and-multiplex tree that is almost certainly on the
critical path, limiting Fmax to 127 MHz (well below the 135 MHz target).
CoreMark is dominated by multiply-add and pointer-chasing; it contains no
dynamic division instructions, so making division multi-cycle has negligible
IPC cost for the fitness benchmark while removing the longest
combinational path.

hypothesis.motivation:
The baseline ALU implements DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU using combinational Verilog
`/` and `%` operators.  On the Tang Nano 20K this infers a massive
combinational subtract-and-multiplex tree that is almost certainly on the
critical path, limiting Fmax to 127 MHz (well below the 135 MHz target).
CoreMark is dominated by multiply-add and pointer-chasing; it contains no
dynamic division instructions, so making division multi-cycle has negligible
IPC cost for the fitness benchmark while removing the longest
combinational path.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Remove DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU from the combinational ALU (non-ALTOPS path only;
keep the ALTOPS XOR substitutions so riscv-formal still matches).  Add a
new clocked iterative divider module (`divider.sv`) that computes a 32-bit
quotient or remainder in 32 cycles using a shift-and-subtract algorithm.
The EX stage detects a division opcode, starts the divider, and stalls the
pipeline (`stall_ex_mem`, `stall_id`, `stall_if`) while `divider.busy` is
high.  When the divider finishes its result is muxed into the EX/MEM
`alu_result` field exactly as before, so downstream MEM/WB logic is
unchanged.  Multiplication stays combinational (mapped to FPGA DSPs) and
is unaffected.  This structural change breaks the worst-case combinational
path and should raise Fmax by 10–20 % with no CoreMark IPC penalty.

changes.description: In the non-ALTOPS (`else`) branch, remove the DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU case arms so the ALU no longer contains combinational division logic.  Keep the `ifdef RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS` XOR substitutions unchanged so formal verification continues to match the spec.

New 32-cycle iterative restoring/non-restoring divider module with `start`, `busy`, `done`, `dividend`, `divisor`, `is_signed`, `rem_op` inputs and a 32-bit `result` output.  Handles the RV32M special cases (divide-by-zero and INT_MIN/-1) inside the module or in a thin wrapper.

Instantiate the divider.  When `in.ctrl.alu_op` is DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU, drive `alu_result` from the divider output instead of the ALU, assert `divider_start`, and keep the EX/MEM register frozen via a new `div_stall` signal until `divider_done` arrives.  Jumps and branches continue to resolve normally; only the division instruction itself is delayed.
Accept a new `div_busy` input.  Assert `stall_if`, `stall_id`, and `stall_ex_mem` whenever `div_busy` is high so the entire pipeline freezes around the in-flight division.

Remove the DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU vectors from the ALU testbench (the ALU no longer implements them).  Add a new `test_divider.py` or extend the existing ALU test to instantiate `divider.sv` and verify exact 32-cycle bit-wise results for all four division operations including the edge cases.

In the non-ALTOPS (`else`) branch, remove the DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU case arms so the ALU no longer contains combinational division logic.  Keep the `ifdef RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS` XOR substitutions unchanged so formal verification continues to match the spec.

New 32-cycle iterative restoring/non-restoring divider module with `start`, `busy`, `done`, `dividend`, `divisor`, `is_signed`, `rem_op` inputs and a 32-bit `result` output.  Handles the RV32M special cases (divide-by-zero and INT_MIN/-1) inside the module or in a thin wrapper.

Instantiate the divider.  When `in.ctrl.alu_op` is DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU, drive `alu_result` from the divider output instead of the ALU, assert `divider_start`, and keep the EX/MEM register frozen via a new `div_stall` signal until `divider_done` arrives.  Jumps and branches continue to resolve normally; only the division instruction itself is delayed.
Accept a new `div_busy` input.  Assert `stall_if`, `stall_id`, and `stall_ex_mem` whenever `div_busy` is high so the entire pipeline freezes around the in-flight division.

Remove the DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU vectors from the ALU testbench (the ALU no longer implements them).  Add a new `test_divider.py` or extend the existing ALU test to instantiate `divider.sv` and verify exact 32-cycle bit-wise results for all four division operations including the edge cases.

title: Static backward-taken branch and JAL predictor
motivation:
The baseline 5-stage core resolves all control flow in EX, incurring a fixed
2-cycle flush penalty for every taken branch, JAL, and JALR. CoreMark spends
most of its time in tight loops whose backward branches are taken >95 % of
the time.  Predicting backward branches as taken and JAL as always taken
eliminates the flush bubble for the common case, improving IPC with only a
few LUTs of combinational logic and no SRAM/BRAM.

hypothesis.motivation:
The baseline 5-stage core resolves all control flow in EX, incurring a fixed
2-cycle flush penalty for every taken branch, JAL, and JALR. CoreMark spends
most of its time in tight loops whose backward branches are taken >95 % of
the time.  Predicting backward branches as taken and JAL as always taken
eliminates the flush bubble for the common case, improving IPC with only a
few LUTs of combinational logic and no SRAM/BRAM.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a single-bit static predictor in the IF stage:
- JAL (opcode 1101111) is always predicted taken (target = PC + J-immediate).
- Backward conditional branches (opcode 1100011 with B-immediate sign bit = 1)
  are predicted taken (target = PC + B-immediate).
- Everything else is predicted not-taken (PC+4).
The prediction bit `pred_taken` is threaded through the IF/ID and ID/EX
bundles.  In EX the actual outcome is compared with the prediction;
`redirect` is asserted only on misprediction.  Correctly-predicted taken
backward branches and JAL instructions therefore retire without any pipeline
flush, removing the 2-cycle penalty that currently hurts CoreMark IPC.

changes.description: Add `logic pred_taken` to `if_id_t` and `id_ex_t` packed structs so the prediction travels with the instruction through the pipeline.

Decode opcode and immediate sign from `imem_data`.  Compute J-target and B-target combinationally.  Assert `predict_taken` for JAL or for backward branches.  Drive `next_pc` to the predicted target when predicted taken, otherwise PC+4.  Output `pred_taken` in the IF/ID bundle (zeroed on flush/redirect).
Capture `in.pred_taken` into the ID/EX register so the prediction reaches the EX stage.

Replace the unconditional `redirect = branch_taken || is_jump` with prediction checking: `pred_correct` is true when (a) the instruction is a jump and `pred_taken==1`, (b) the instruction is a branch and `branch_taken == pred_taken`, or (c) the instruction is neither.  Assert `redirect` only when `!pred_correct && !misalign_fault`.  The redirect target is computed exactly as before (branch target, jump target, or PC+4).
Add `logic pred_taken` to `if_id_t` and `id_ex_t` packed structs so the prediction travels with the instruction through the pipeline.

Decode opcode and immediate sign from `imem_data`.  Compute J-target and B-target combinationally.  Assert `predict_taken` for JAL or for backward branches.  Drive `next_pc` to the predicted target when predicted taken, otherwise PC+4.  Output `pred_taken` in the IF/ID bundle (zeroed on flush/redirect).
Capture `in.pred_taken` into the ID/EX register so the prediction reaches the EX stage.

Replace the unconditional `redirect = branch_taken || is_jump` with prediction checking: `pred_correct` is true when (a) the instruction is a jump and `pred_taken==1`, (b) the instruction is a branch and `branch_taken == pred_taken`, or (c) the instruction is neither.  Assert `redirect` only when `!pred_correct && !misalign_fault`.  The redirect target is computed exactly as before (branch target, jump target, or PC+4).
